name: Two.js (2D Drawing API for Canvas/SVG/WebGL)

performance.notes: Renderer-dependent; WebGL fastest, SVG slowest for many objects

agent_readiness.notes: Renderer-agnostic 2D API (Canvas/SVG/WebGL). const two = new Two({ width: 500, height: 400 }).appendTo(document.body). const circle = two.makeCircle(200, 200, 50). const rect = two.makeRectangle(100, 100, 200, 100). circle.fill = '#FF8000'. circle.stroke = 'black'. const path = two.makePath([new Two.Vector(0,0), ...]). two.update() to render. two.bind('update', function() {...}). two.play() for animation loop. Shapes: makeCircle, makeEllipse, makeRectangle, makePolygon, makeStar, makeLine, makePoints, makeCurve, makePath. Groups: two.makeGroup(shape1, shape2). Tween: external GSAP integration. No built-in text.

gotchas: three.js is a different 3D library — don't confuse with two.js (2D only)
No text rendering built-in — use SVG text or HTML overlay
Documentation quality is mediocre — examples are primary learning resource
No type annotations — limited IDE support
Renderer switching requires recreation of Two instance
best_when: 2D graphics needing renderer flexibility (Canvas, SVG, WebGL) with same API
When PixiJS is too heavy and p5.js is too educational
avoid_when: Large teams — documentation is lacking vs p5.js or PixiJS
summary: Two.js is a renderer-agnostic 2D drawing API (Canvas/SVG/WebGL). new Two({width, height}).appendTo(el). makeCircle(), makeRectangle(), makePath(). fill, stroke properties. two.play() for animation. Groups. MIT by Jono Brandel. Best for renderer-flexible 2D graphics. No built-in text. Documentation mediocre. No type annotations. Use p5.js for creative coding, PixiJS for performance, Three.js for 3D.
